title: Central Limit Theorem
intuition: No matter what distribution X comes from (as long as it has finite variance), the standardised sample mean converges to standard normal as n → ∞. This is why the normal distribution appears everywhere.
pitfalls: The CLT requires finite variance — it fails for heavy-tailed distributions like the Cauchy.
'Converges in distribution' does not mean converges almost surely.
The approximation quality depends on n and the shape of F.
references: Billingsley, P. (1995). Probability and Measure. 3rd ed.
Durrett, R. (2019). Probability: Theory and Examples. 5th ed.

derivationSteps.explanation: Define the standardised mean Z_n. Let Y_i = (X_i - μ)/σ, so E[Y_i]=0, Var(Y_i)=1, and Z_n = (1/√n) Σ Y_i.
hints: Working with standardised variables Y_i = (X_i-μ)/σ simplifies the algebra significantly.

derivationSteps.explanation: Since E[Y_i]=0 and E[Y_i²]=1, the MGF of Y_i satisfies M_{Y_i}(t) = E[e^{tY_i}] = 1 + t·E[Y_i] + (t²/2)·E[Y_i²] + O(t³) = 1 + t²/2 + O(t³).

derivationSteps.justification: MGF of Sum of Independent Variables
derivationSteps.explanation: Since Y_i are i.i.d., the MGF of Z_n = (1/√n)ΣY_i is the n-th power of the MGF of Y_1/√n, which equals M_{Y_1}(t/√n).
Replace M_{Y_1}(t/√n) with its Taylor expansion at t/√n: 1 + (t/√n)²/2 + O((t/√n)³) = 1 + t²/(2n) + O(n^{-3/2}).
Using the fundamental limit lim_{n→∞}(1 + x/n)^n = e^x with x = t²/2.
hints: This is the standard limit e^x = lim_{n→∞}(1+x/n)^n.

derivationSteps.explanation: The MGF of N(0,1) is e^{t²/2} (obtained by setting μ=0, σ²=1 in the normal MGF). Since MGFs uniquely determine distributions, Z_n →^d N(0,1).
By the continuity theorem, pointwise convergence of MGFs in a neighbourhood of 0 implies convergence in distribution. Since M_{Z_n}(t) → e^{t²/2} for all t ∈ ℝ, Z_n →^d N(0,1).

examples.title: Dice Rolling
examples.setup: Roll a fair die n=30 times. What is the approximate distribution of the sample mean?

title: MLE for Normal Distribution Parameters
intuition: We find the parameter values that make the observed data most probable. For the normal distribution, the sample mean is the MLE for μ, but the MLE for σ² is biased (divides by n, not n-1).
pitfalls: The MLE σ̂² is biased — the unbiased estimator uses n-1 in the denominator (Bessel's correction).
references: Casella, G. & Berger, R. L. (2002). Statistical Inference. 2nd ed. Chapter 7.

derivationSteps.explanation: By i.i.d., the joint density factors as a product. This is the likelihood function L(μ,σ²) viewed as a function of parameters for fixed data x₁,…,xₙ.
Taking log converts the product to a sum. The log-likelihood ℓ = log L is easier to maximise.
hints: Maximising ℓ is equivalent to maximising L since log is monotone increasing.

derivationSteps.explanation: Setting the partial derivative with respect to μ equal to zero. The term -n/2·ln(2π) and -n/2·ln(σ²) vanish.
Σ(xᵢ - μ) = 0 implies nμ = Σxᵢ, so μ̂ = x̄. The second derivative is -n/σ² < 0, confirming this is a maximum.
Setting the partial derivative with respect to σ² equal to zero. Note: differentiate with respect to σ², treating it as a single parameter.
Multiplying by 2σ⁴/n: σ² = (1/n)Σ(xᵢ-μ)². Substituting μ̂ = x̄ gives the MLE for σ². Note: this divides by n (biased).
hints: The unbiased sample variance uses n-1. MLE naturally produces the biased version.

title: Cramér–Rao Lower Bound
assumptions: The Fisher information I(\theta) is finite and positive
Regularity conditions hold (differentiation under the integral sign is valid)
intuition: No unbiased estimator can have variance smaller than 1/I(θ). Fisher information measures how much information the data carries about θ — more information means tighter estimates are possible.
pitfalls: The CRLB applies to unbiased estimators. For biased estimators, the bound involves the derivative of the bias.
Regularity conditions (interchange of differentiation and integration) must hold.
references: Cramér, H. (1946). Mathematical Methods of Statistics.

derivationSteps.explanation: Differentiating the normalisation condition ∫f(x;θ)dx = 1 with respect to θ (valid by regularity) and rearranging gives E[∂_θ ln f] = 0.
Differentiating E[θ̂] = ∫θ̂(x)f(x;θ)dx with respect to θ and using ∂_θ f = f · ∂_θ ln f gives ∂_θ E[θ̂] = E[θ̂ · s(X;θ)] where s = ∂_θ ln f is the score.
Since E[s]=0 (step 1), Cov(θ̂, s) = E[θ̂·s] - E[θ̂]E[s] = E[θ̂·s] = ∂_θ E[θ̂].
Applying Cauchy-Schwarz: [Cov(A,B)]² ≤ Var(A)·Var(B) for any two random variables A, B.
The Fisher information I(θ) = E[s²] = Var(s) since E[s] = 0. This is the variance of the score function.
Rearranging step 4: Var(θ̂) ≥ [∂_θ E[θ̂]]² / I(θ). For unbiased θ̂, E[θ̂] = θ so ∂_θ E[θ̂] = 1, giving Var(θ̂) ≥ 1/I(θ).
